behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions (month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with descriptive errors.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is deterministic parsing/validation logic and error construction inside local code. No I/O, OS, network, subprocess, or model behavior is required. A pure function/object test can catch real failures here.
behaviors.test_description: Call the parser directly with invalid cron strings (`0 0 * 13 *`, `0 0 32 * *`, malformed token patterns). Assert parse fails, error type/category matches validation class, and message includes the offending field/value so operators can diagnose quickly.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron, including last-day-of-month and nearest-weekday edge cases.
behaviors.justification: Real failures often occur at local module handoffs (cron expression parsing/normalization -> schedule object -> next-run calculator). A unit test of only one function can miss semantic mismatches for `L`/nearest-weekday handling even without external infra.
behaviors.test_description: Use real parser + schedule object + run-time calculator together. From fixed start times, compute next N runs for cases like `0 9 L * *` and nearest-weekday variants, and compare against known expected timestamps across month boundaries and leap-year months.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector flags schedules firing within a configured minimum gap and reports conflicting windows.
behaviors.justification: This depends on composed local behavior: schedule expansion + temporal comparison + conflict-window reporting. Mocking schedule outputs can hide real boundary bugs in time normalization and window construction, so multi-module local wiring is the minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Create two real schedules through the parser/calculator, generate occurrences in a bounded horizon, run overlap detection with a 5-minute gap, and assert detected conflict windows match exact expected start/end windows (including no false positives outside the gap).

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling is correct across DST spring-forward and fall-back for a 2:30 AM job.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real timezone/DST rule behavior from runtime/OS tzdata. In-process mocks commonly hide real failures around nonexistent/ambiguous local times, so this crosses into OS-behavior validation.
behaviors.test_description: Run engine end-to-end with real timezone data (e.g., `America/New_York`) around concrete DST dates. For spring-forward, verify 2:30 AM nonexistent-time policy is applied correctly; for fall-back, verify ambiguous 2:30 AM handling (single or duplicate fire per spec) is correct and stable.

behaviors.description: When next run is already in the past, engine applies missed-run policy (skip vs backfill).
behaviors.justification: Behavior requires local coordination across scheduler state/time source, next-run computation, and policy logic. A single-function unit test can miss real policy-application and state-transition defects; no external network/LLM is needed.
behaviors.test_description: With a controllable clock and persisted/seeded last-run state, simulate downtime so computed next run is in the past. Execute scheduling decision flow for `skip` and `backfill` policies, asserting emitted run set, updated next-run pointer, and idempotent behavior on repeated evaluation.
